coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots at low temperature for cellular labeling
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Yuqian
Gao, Hui
Lai, Luhao
Li, Xiaolong
coredata.dc:description: A facile one-step approach is developed for the large-scale synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQDs) at low temperature by using citric acid (CA) as the precursor. The emission wavelength of the N-GQDs is nearly excitation-dependent, which may mirror the effects from particles of different sizes and distribution of different emissive sites on nanoparticles. Moreover, the products are then tested with live Hela cells for the extraordinary biocompatibility. The cellular viability is remained to be 100% even after a 48 h exposure with the high N-GQDs concentration of 200 μg mL−1 and this survival rate is really much higher than that have been reported previously. The as-prepared N-GQDs with negligible inherent toxicity exhibit excellent biological applications such as bio-imaging and cell tracking.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cellular labeling
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-GQDs and cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The (a) TEM images, (b) size distribution, (c) FT-IR and (d) UV spectra of N-GQDs.
(a) The PL spectra and (b) CIE chromaticity coordinate of NS-CDs under a series of excitation wavelength.

(a) The luminescence decay spectra of NS-CDs. The fluorescence images (370 nm excitation) of HeLa cells after the incubation with N-GQDs for (b) 4 h and (c) 24 h and (d) the effect of N-GQDs on HeLa cells viability.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of N-GQDs and proposed mechanism for endocytosis of N-GQDs enters cells.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots at low temperature for cellular labeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility, Shanghai Institute of Applied Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201204, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to the article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile one-step approach is developed for the large-scale synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQDs) at low temperature by using citric acid (CA) as the precursor. The emission wavelength of the N-GQDs is nearly excitation-dependent, which may mirror the effects from particles of different sizes and distribution of different emissive sites on nanoparticles. Moreover, the products are then tested with live Hela cells for the extraordinary biocompatibility. The cellular viability is remained to be 100% even after a 48 h exposure with the high N-GQDs concentration of 200 μg mL−1 and this survival rate is really much higher than that have been reported previously. The as-prepared N-GQDs with negligible inherent toxicity exhibit excellent biological applications such as bio-imaging and cell tracking.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs), nanodiamonds, carbon nanotubes, fullerene and especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [1], have attracted tremendous research interests owing to their excellent optical properties, chemical stability, and simple synthetic routes [2,3]. The superior biological and electronic properties of carbon-based quantum dots as electron donors and acceptors and electrochemical luminescence, endow them with wide potentials in optronics, catalysis and sensors [4]. For GQDs, they generally possess one or a few layers of graphene and connected chemical groups on the edges, in addition, they are anisotropic with lateral dimensions larger than their height which make them different from the other carbon materials in structure [4–6]. The GQDs with the existence of a carbon core and certain crystallinity also reveal low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [6–8]. These superiorities make the GQDs a perfect candidate for the numerous promising applications in bio-imaging and bio-sensing [9]. Unlike the conventional semiconductor quantum dots such as CdO and CsPbI3, the utilizing of toxic heavy metals could cause severe health and environment hazards and restrict their further applications in biology [10,11]. The highly efficient and environmentally benign GQDs are mainly composed of non-toxic C, O, and N elements and exhibit a detectable fluorescence in the live cells.

Owing to these advantages of GQDs, there are many approaches to prepare them which are mainly named “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods by distinguishing from the exclusively organic routes [12,13]. The “top-down” method is generally referring to cut carbon resources such as carbon fibers and carbon nanotubes into GQDs while the “bottom-up” methods is meaning to synthesis the GQDs from small organic molecules or polymers. The N-GQDs in this experiment is fabricated by the hydrothermal process belonging to “top-down” method which could primely modify the surface functional groups [14,15].

Based on the plentiful preparation methods [16], considerable efforts have been focused on changing the functional groups on the edge of the GQDs at present time. The functional groups introduced by the doped atoms, especially nitrogen atoms, can coordinate the intrinsic electronic properties and surface chemical reactivities of the GQDs [17–20]. Some reporters demonstrated that only the nitrogen bonding to carbon can really enhance the photoluminescence (PL) intensities with multicolor and two-photon up-conversion properties and the N-GQDs even show that the PL intensities is nitrogen content-dependent [1,21]. Recently, the N-GQDs for bioimaging was reported by Zhou's group and the N-GQDs was synthesized from layered Ti3C2Tx MXene in dimethylformamide (DMF). The as-prepared N-GQDs with superior properties of bright and tunable photoluminescence and low cytotoxicity are promising for applications in cellular imaging [22]. Herein, we report a facile and economical method to prepare the strong fluorescent N-GQDs by magnetic stirring pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) at low temperature. Except for the good luminescence stability, the live cells also keep a high survival rate in the as-prepared N-GQDs solution which endows the resultant N-GQDs as the ideal contrast media for cellular labeling and bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-GQDs and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: 9.607 g CA and 1 ml EDA were dissolved into 100 ml deionized water in a conical flask. Then the mixture was thoroughly stirred for ten minutes in room temperature until it became a homogeneous solution. The final N-GQDs sample would be obtained after stirring on a magnetic stirrer at 70 °C for 30 h. In the followed steps, Hela cells (the concentration of 2*105/ hole) are plated on a 30-mm Petri dish in culture medium one day in advance for cell imaging. For in vitro study, cells are incubated in medium containing N-GQDs for 24 h, then treated with PBS for two times to wash the unabsorbed free dots. The whole system was imaged on laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM-710).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is performed using an F-30 S-TWIN electron microscope (Tecnai G2, FEI Company). Deionized water is gained by a Millipore Elix 5 UV and Milli-Q Gradient Ultra-Pure Water System. The Fourier transform infra-red spectra (FT-IR) of the samples are recorded between 500 and 4000 cm-1 on a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet NEXUS 670) using KBr pellets. The PL spectra and fluorescence emission spectra are recorded using an FLS-920T fluorescence spectrophotometer and a HORIBA JOBIN YVON Fluorolog-3 Spectro fluorometer system at 16 °C, respectively. Without particular statement, all the measurements are performed at room temperature.

The cytotoxicity is assessed using the classic MTT assay with Hela cells. The liquid culture is poured to leave the adherent culture of Hela cells digested by E enzyme. After the whole digestion process, the cells are completely separated from the wall and dispersed well evenly after being percussed by 3 ml serum containing medium 1640. The cell suspensions with suitable concentration and certain volume are seeded into a 96-well plate with ∼5 × 103 cells per well respectively by the counting plate. The cells are incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 12 h in a humidified atmosphere in the followed steps. The cells are then incubated in the various concentrations (10, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 μg/mL) of N-GQDs for different time in the wells. What is more, 20 μL MTT (5 mg/mL) is added into the wells and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, the dark blue formazan crystals would be left at the bottom of the hole when the culture solution is evaporated. Then dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO) is added to dissolve the formazan completely. Parallel detection of 4 holes in each experiment are carried twice.

Fig.1 (a) and (b) show the transmission electron microscopy image and size distributions of the N-GQDs, it reveals that the resulting spherical products with a relatively narrow size distribution (from 2.3 nm to 5.0 nm) exhibit a good water solubility in the deionized water. The inset of Fig. 1(a) shows the representative high-resolution TEM image of an individual dot with a clear lattice image and it indicates the high crystalline quality of the samples. In addition, the lattice spacing of 0.31 nm is corresponding to the (100) spacing of graphitic carbon lattice parameters11. The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 1(c) reveals the changes of the chemical functional groups. The broad peak at 3434 cm−1 is corresponded to the OH and NH, which are mainly belonged to the marginal functional groups such as carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups. Besides that, the FT-IR spectrum of the as-made N-GQDs shows a CO (carbony/carboxyl) stretching peak at 1716 cm-1 and CO (epoxy) stretching peak at 1223 cm−1. In addition, the peaks at 1407 cm−1 and 1621 cm−1 can be identified as CN, NH and CC groups, respectively [23,24]. What is more, the figure of XPS in Fig. S1 (a) furtherly provides the composition of elements and their chemical states and indicates that the N atoms have been doped into the N-GQDs successfully. As shown in the high resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S1 (b)), distinct peaks at 284.9 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.6 eV and 288.8 eV are attributed to the CC, CN, CN (CO) and COOH, respectively. Besides that, the high resolution spectrum of N 1s peak (Fig. S1 (c)) reveals that the nitrogen mainly exists in the form of pyrrolic-N (∼ 400.4 eV), and there are also some NH (∼ 401.7 eV) groups within the resultant carbonaceous structure. The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs are shown in Fig.1(d), the absorption peak at 255 nm reveals the typical π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. Excitation-emission measurements are shown in Fig. 2 (a) for characterizing the energy levels of N-GQDs, and the emission wavelength is nearly excitation-dependent, which is always a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials [25]. The emission peaks of the N-GQDs have a red-shift to longer wavelengths (from 466 nm to 529 nm) when the excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 440 nm, in addition, the products have the maximum emission wavelength when excited with 370 nm light. The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) in Fig. 2(b) also reveals this visual phenomenon. These points represent the positions of the excitation under different wavelengths, respectively, and the CIE coordinate calculated as (0.1924, 0.2565) displays the area of maximum emission wavelength under 370 nm excitation. With the excitation wavelength increasing, the emission position ranges from blue light to green-yellow light area. These behaviors may reflect not only the effects from particles of different sizes in the N-GQDs, but also the distribution of different emissive sites on nanoparticles. The spectrum of luminescence decay is shown in Fig. 3 (a), and the decay time is recorded for the N-GQDs transitions at 466 nm for blue emission and 360 nm excitation measured by a time-correlated single photon counting technique, and the observed lifetimes are τ1 = 3.65 ns and τ2 = 8.55 ns, which are similar to that of the reported GQDs [26]. In addition, the spectra of decay time under a series of different excitation wavelengths are supplemented in the Fig. S2. It reveals that the decay time of the N-GQDs is nearly excitation-independent. The N-GQDs exhibit the similar decay time under different excitation wavelengths (including 330 nm: τ1 = 3.52 ns and τ2 = 8.15 ns, 360 nm: τ1 = 3.65 ns and τ2 = 7.81 ns and 405 nm: τ1 = 3.18 ns and τ2 = 8.28 ns).

Toxicity studies have been reported by various research groups previously which show that GQDs appear to have low toxicity [27,28]. Hence, the cytotoxicity of N-GQDs is further evaluated by the MTT assay with HeLa cells. Fig. 3(d) exhibits the cell viability studies under various concentrations of N-GQDs. The relative cell viability is remained to be 100% even after a 48 h exposure with the N-GQDs concentrations of 10, 100 and 200 μg mL−1. The survival rate is really much higher than that have been reported by X. Gong et al. (survival rate: 82%, concentration: 100 μg mL−1) and S. Dong et al. (survival rate: 84%, concentration: 200 μg mL−1) [25,29]. While in the followed steps, cell viability gradually decreases to 69% and 19% with the increasing of the concentration of N-GQDs to 500 and 1000 μg ml−1. The results suggest that the N-GQDs have very low toxicity to the HeLa cells at a relatively low concentration, indicating its potential biological applications. Considering the good biocompatibility of the N-GQDs, the N-GQDs is then introduced into the HeLa cells for in vitro bio-imaging using confocal microscopy (Fig. 3(b) and (c)). The cells initially incubated with N-GQDs for 4 and 24 h respectively display blue emissions when they are excited at 370 nm laser and the emissions mainly occurred in the areas of the cell membrane and cytoplasm, especially around the cell nucleus. These phenomena demonstrate the successful translocation of the N-GQDs through the cell membrane by cellular endocytosis (Fig. 4 ). These confocal images indicate that the as-prepared N-GQDs exhibit stable photoluminescence, excellent cyto-compatibility and could be used as a potential probe for cellular imaging and biological fields.

In conclusion, we developed a low-cost and facile method to synthesize the N-GQDs on a large scale using CA and EDA as the precursors. The FT-IR spectrum shows that the N element has been doped into the N-GQDs successfully. Besides that, the high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s also reveals that the nitrogen atoms are mainly existing in the form of pyrrolic-N, and there are also some NH groups within the resultant carbonaceous structure. More importantly, the obtained N-GQDs exhibit good luminescence stability, biocompatibility and especially low toxicity. The cellular viability is remained to be 100% even after a 48 h exposure with the high N-GQDs concentration of 200 μg mL−1 and it is anticipated that the N-GQDs would exhibit potential applications in biological labeling and bio-imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51402140, U1632129) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2016-128).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2018.03.053.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
